paraphrase: The Académie André Delvaux organized the 4th Magritte Awards ceremony, an event that recognized the most outstanding films produced in Belgium during 2013. This celebration occurred on February 1, 2014, commencing at 8:00 p.m. CET, at the Square, located within the historical Mont des Arts area in Brussels. Throughout the event, the Académie André Delvaux bestowed Magritte Awards across a total of 21 distinct categories. BeTV broadcasted the ceremony live to audiences throughout Belgium. Actress Émilie Dequenne took on the role of president for the ceremony, while actor Fabrizio Rongione served as the host for the second occasion. The list of nominees for the 4th Magritte Awards was made public on January 9, 2014. Among the nominated films, "Tango libre" received the highest number of nominations with ten, closely followed by "In the Name of the Son" which garnered seven nominations. The identities of the award recipients were unveiled during the actual awards ceremony on February 1, 2014. "Ernest & Celestine" also featured prominently.

original: The 2014 UEC European Track Championships was the fifth edition of the elite UEC European Track Championships in track cycling and took place at the Vélodrome Amédée Détraux in Baie-Mahault, Guadeloupe, France, between 16 and 19 October. The Event was organised by the European Cycling Union. All European champions are awarded the UEC European Champion jersey which may be worn by the champion throughout the year when competing in the same event at other competitions. paraphrase: The 2014 UEC European Track Championships, the fifth iteration of the premier UEC European Track Championships for track cycling, transpired from October 16 to 19 at the Vélodrome Amédée Détraux in Baie-Mahault, Guadeloupe, France. The European Cycling Union was responsible for organizing this event. Every European champion is presented with the UEC European Champion jersey, a garment they are permitted to wear for the entire year when participating in the identical event at various other competitions. The schedule for the 2014 championships saw a substantial expansion, encompassing all World Championship track events. This included ten Olympic events—specifically sprint, team sprint, keirin, team pursuit, and omnium, for both male and female competitors—where qualification points for the 2016 Summer Olympics became available for the inaugural time, alongside nine non-Olympic events.

original: The 2023 Rey de Reyes (Spanish for "2023 King of Kings") was a professional wrestling tournament and supercard event produced by the Mexican Lucha Libre AAA Worldwide (AAA) promotion. The event was held on February 5, 2023, at Poliforum Zamna in Merida, Yucatan, Mexico. The 2023 event was the 26th Rey de Reyes show and tournament. In the main event match, Sam Adonis defeated Bandido, Pagano, and Hijo del Vikingo to become the 2023 Rey de Reyes. In other prominent matches, Sexy Star II defeated Chik Tormenta, Lady Shani, La Hiedra, and Dalys in a five-way match to become the 2023 Reina de Reinas and Negro Casas made his AAA in-ring debut, losing to Hijo del Vikingo in a Rey de Reyes qualifier that also involved Myzteziz Jr.
paraphrase: The 2023 Rey de Reyes, which translates to "2023 King of Kings" in Spanish, constituted a professional wrestling competition and a major event presented by the Mexican Lucha Libre AAA Worldwide (AAA) organization. This spectacle took place on February 5, 2023, at the Poliforum Zamna, located in Merida, Yucatan, Mexico. The 2023 iteration marked the twenty-sixth occasion of the Rey de Reyes show and its accompanying tournament. Within the concluding main event bout, Sam Adonis emerged victorious over Bandido, Pagano, and Hijo del Vikingo, thereby securing the title of the 2023 Rey de Reyes. Among other notable contests, Sexy Star II bested Chik Tormenta, Lady Shani, La Hiedra, and Dalys in a five-person match to claim the 2023 Reina de Reinas title. Additionally, Negro Casas had his inaugural in-ring appearance for AAA, where he suffered a defeat at the hands of Hijo del Vikingo in a Rey de Reyes qualifying match that also featured Myzteziz Jr.

original: Parliamentary elections were held in Estonia on 5 March 2023 to elect all 101 members of the Riigikogu. The officially published election data indicate the victory of the Reform Party, which won 37 seats in total, while the Conservative People's Party of Estonia (EKRE) placed second with 17 seats. The Centre Party won 16 seats, a loss of 10, while Estonia 200 won 14 seats, gaining representation in Riigikogu. After the previous parliamentary election in 2019, the Centre Party, led by Jüri Ratas, formed a government with Ratas serving as prime minister. His government was brought down in January 2021 after a corruption investigation, and Kaja Kallas of the Reform Party formed a coalition government with the Centre Party; which collapsed in June 2022. paraphrase: On March 5, 2023, Estonia conducted parliamentary elections to choose all 101 representatives for the Riigikogu. The officially released election results confirmed the triumph of the Reform Party, which secured a total of 37 seats. The Conservative People's Party of Estonia (EKRE) finished in second place, obtaining 17 seats. The Centre Party acquired 16 seats, representing a decrease of 10 seats compared to the previous election, while Estonia 200 gained 14 seats, thereby achieving representation in the Riigikogu for the first time. Following the preceding parliamentary election in 2019, the Centre Party, under the leadership of Jüri Ratas, established a government with Ratas as the prime minister. This government subsequently dissolved in January 2021 due to a corruption inquiry. Consequently, Kaja Kallas, representing the Reform Party, formed a coalition government in collaboration with the Centre Party; however, this alliance disintegrated in June 2022. Kallas subsequently proceeded to form a new government.

original: The Delhi-Kathmandu bus is a trans-boundary bus service connecting India and Nepal's capitals at Delhi and Kathmandu respectively. The service is operated by the Delhi Transport Corporation (DTC) and was launched in 2014.While Delhi Transport Corporation (DTC), a state run corporation, will run the bus service from Indian side, the Federation of Nepalese National Transport Entrepreneurs (FNNTE) is operating the bus service under supervision of the Ministry of Physical Infrastructure and Transport MoPIT. Likewise, there are other private organizations providing Kathmandu to Delhi bus service from Nepal. This bus service has a 2X2 seating arrangement and is an AC bus service. The service operates from Dr. Ambedkar bus stand near Delhi gate in Delhi. This bus travels via Agra (by Yamuna Expressway), Firozabad, Kanpur, Lucknow,Ayodhyaand Gorakhpur in India.
paraphrase: The trans-boundary bus service known as the Delhi-Kathmandu bus establishes a connection between the capital cities of India and Nepal, specifically Delhi and Kathmandu. This service is under the management of the Delhi Transport Corporation (DTC) and commenced operations in 2014. The Delhi Transport Corporation (DTC), which is a state-owned corporation, is responsible for running the bus service from the Indian side. Concurrently, the Federation of Nepalese National Transport Entrepreneurs (FNNTE) oversees the operation of the bus service in Nepal, with the guidance of the Ministry of Physical Infrastructure and Transport (MoPIT). Furthermore, several other private entities offer bus services from Kathmandu to Delhi within Nepal. This particular bus service offers a 2X2 seating configuration and is equipped with air conditioning. The departure point for this service is the Dr. Ambedkar bus stand, situated near Delhi Gate in Delhi. The route taken by this bus includes stops in Agra (utilizing the Yamuna Expressway), Firozabad, Kanpur, Lucknow, Ayodhya, and Gorakhpur, all located within India.

original: From January 28 to February 4, 2023, a Chinese-operated high-altitude balloon was spotted in North American airspace, including Alaska, western Canada, and the contiguous United States. On February 4, the U.S. Air Force shot down the balloon over U.S. territorial waters off the coast of South Carolina, on the order of U.S. President Joe Biden. It was shot down by a U.S. F-22 Raptor. Debris from the wreckage was recovered and sent to the FBI Laboratory in Quantico, Virginia, for analysis.The American and Canadian militaries announced that the balloon was for surveillance, while the Chinese government maintained it was a civilian (mainly meteorological) airship that had been blown off course. The U.S. said that the balloon was capable of geolocating electronic communications and carried intelligence surveillance equipment inconsistent
paraphrase: Between January 28 and February 4, 2023, a high-altitude balloon, operated by China, was observed traversing the airspace of North America, encompassing regions such as Alaska, western Canada, and the contiguous United States. Subsequently, on February 4, acting under the directive of U.S. President Joe Biden, the U.S. Air Force successfully downed the balloon above the territorial waters of the United States, situated off the South Carolina coastline. The takedown was executed by a U.S. F-22 Raptor. Fragments from the downed craft were retrieved and subsequently dispatched to the FBI Laboratory in Quantico, Virginia, for thorough examination. Both the American and Canadian armed forces asserted that the balloon's purpose was surveillance. In contrast, the Chinese government insisted that it was a civilian airship, primarily for meteorological purposes, which had inadvertently deviated from its intended flight path. The United States further stated that the balloon possessed the capability to geolocate electronic communications and was equipped with intelligence surveillance apparatus that contradicted the civilian explanation.

The film nominations were announced live via YouTube on November 22, 2022 by Raúl Castillo and Taylour Paige, while the television nominations were announced on December 13, 2022 by Asia Kate Dillon. paraphrase: On March 4, 2023, Film Independent hosted the 38th Film Independent Spirit Awards, an event dedicated to recognizing the most outstanding independent movies and television programs from the year 2022. The nominees for films were revealed during a live YouTube broadcast on November 22, 2022, with Raúl Castillo and Taylour Paige making the announcements. Subsequently, on December 13, 2022, Asia Kate Dillon unveiled the television nominations. Following the pattern established last year, the awards ceremony was no longer held on the day immediately preceding the Academy Awards, a departure from its historical timing. Instead, a period of eight days separated the two events, as the 95th Academy Awards were scheduled for March 12, 2023. This particular awards show unfolded amidst the crucial final voting period for the Oscars, which spanned from March 2 to March 7. Consequently, the victors at this year's Independent Spirit Awards potentially influenced the ultimate outcomes of the Academy Awards.

Voters will be asked whether the Climate Protection and Energy Transition Act of 2016 should be amended. original: The FATCA agreement is an international agreement signed between Canada and the United States that allows the implementation of the Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (an Act of the U.S. Congress) in Canada. paraphrase: The Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA) agreement represents a pact between Canada and the United States, facilitating the enforcement of this U.S. Congressional Act within Canadian borders. This specific agreement is one of thirty such intergovernmental accords that the United States has forged with various nations to enact FATCA. FATCA mandates that United States citizens, encompassing those residing beyond the country's borders, and financial institutions situated outside the United States, disclose the primary balance maintained in their non-U.S. financial accounts to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS). As reported by the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation (CBC), approximately one million American citizens currently reside in Canada. Despite several offers to adopt Marius and an online petition to save him, he was killed on 9 February 2014. paraphrase: Marius, a male giraffe residing at Copenhagen Zoo, was born on February 6, 2012, and died on February 9, 2014. Despite being in good health, he was deemed genetically inappropriate for future breeding programs within captivity because his genetic makeup was too prevalent among the captive giraffe population. Consequently, the zoo's administration made the decision to humanely end his life. In spite of numerous proposals to take Marius in and an online appeal advocating for his preservation, he was put down on February 9, 2014. Subsequently, his remains were publicly dissected and examined during an educational presentation, after which they were given to the zoo's lions as food. This incident garnered extensive global media attention and elicited reactions from various organizations and private citizens, even leading to threats against the lives of the zoo's personnel. From the early 1900s, when data collection commenced, until February 2014, the date of Marius's death, five giraffes had been euthanized for comparable "conservation" purposes.

paraphrase: In March 2023, Germany experienced a country-wide industrial action known as the 2023 German public transport strike. This strike was coordinated by two prominent transportation unions, Ver.di and EVG, whose primary demand was a 10.5% wage increment for their constituents. This significant pay raise was sought to counteract the escalating expenses of food and energy. Representing one of the most substantial industrial actions to impact Germany in multiple decades, the strike had widespread effects, disrupting operations at various airports, including those situated in Munich, Frankfurt, and Hamburg. Additionally, public transportation networks and Germany's largest seaport were also affected. As a consequence, all railroad services, alongside regional and commuter rail services typically operating in the early morning hours, were halted across the entire nation. original: The 2013–2014 Thai political crisis was a period of political instability in Thailand. Anti-government protests took place between November 2013 and May 2014, organised by the People's Democratic Reform Committee (PDRC), a political pressure group led by former Democrat Party parliamentary representative (MP) Suthep Thaugsuban. The crisis eventually resulted in the removal of incumbent Prime Minister Yingluck Shinawatra, a coup d'état, and the establishment of a military junta. The primary aim of the protests was the removal of former prime minister Thaksin Shinawatra's – the brother of Yingluck – influence on Thai politics and the creation of an unelected "people's council" to oversee reforms of the political system. paraphrase: A phase of political upheaval in Thailand, known as the 2013–2014 Thai political crisis, transpired during this time. Demonstrations against the government were organized by the People's Democratic Reform Committee (PDRC), a political advocacy group spearheaded by Suthep Thaugsuban, a former Member of Parliament (MP) for the Democrat Party, and occurred between November 2013 and May 2014. This crisis ultimately culminated in the ousting of the then-current Prime Minister, Yingluck Shinawatra, a military takeover, and the subsequent formation of a military government. The main objective of these protests was to eradicate the influence of former prime minister Thaksin Shinawatra – who was Yingluck's brother – from Thai politics and to establish an appointed "people's council" that would supervise modifications to the political framework. Despite enjoying considerable backing in numerous regions of Thailand, protesters perceived Thaksin as dishonest and detrimental to the democratic process in Thailand.

paraphrase: Operation Lava Jato, known in Portuguese as Operação Lava Jato, constituted a criminal inquiry undertaken by the Curitiba division of Brazil's Federal Police. The investigation commenced in March 2014 and was initially overseen by the investigative judge Sérgio Moro, with Judge Luiz Antônio Bonat assuming leadership in 2019. This extensive probe has led to the issuance of over one thousand warrants covering various categories. As reported by the Operation Car Wash task force, the investigations have implicated administrative personnel within the state-owned oil enterprise Petrobras, political figures from Brazil's most prominent parties (including former presidents of the Republic), presidents of both the Chamber of Deputies and the Federal Senate, state governors, and business executives from significant Brazilian corporations. The Federal Police regards this as the most substantial corruption investigation ever conducted in the nation's history. The specialized task force was formally dissolved on February 1, 2021. What began as an inquiry into money laundering subsequently broadened its scope to encompass a wider array of illicit activities.

Only four candidates contested the elections, the lowest number since 1997. paraphrase: Croatia conducted its sixth presidential elections since achieving independence in 1991, with the voting taking place on December 28, 2014, and January 11, 2015. These elections saw the smallest number of contenders since 1997, with only four individuals vying for the presidency. The then-current president, Ivo Josipović, who had secured his first term as the Social Democratic Party's representative in the 2009–2010 elections, sought to be re-elected for a conclusive five-year term, this time running as an independent candidate. Given that no candidate managed to secure more than 50% of the votes during the initial round of voting in December 2014, a second, decisive round was held in January 2015. This run-off pitted the two candidates who garnered the most votes against each other: Josipović and Kolinda Grabar-Kitarović. Ultimately, Grabar-Kitarović emerged victorious, securing the presidency with a narrow lead of 32,509 votes, which translated to a margin of 1.48%. Her win marked a historic moment, as she became the first female president of Croatia.

On November 11, 2014, a special election, known as a by-election, occurred in the Mitiaro electoral district of the Cook Islands. The initial general election in July 2014 for the Mitiaro parliamentary seat concluded with an equal number of votes for the two leading candidates. Tangata Vavia, the sitting Member of Parliament representing the Democratic Party, secured 50 votes, a total matched precisely by Tuakeu Tangatapoto, his opponent from the Cook Islands Party. A subsequent recount verified this deadlock, which then led the High Court to mandate a new election.

The same two individuals who contested the general election also competed in the subsequent by-election. Although the by-election proceeded as scheduled, the tabulation of votes was postponed due to a legal objection filed by the Cook Islands Party. paraphrase: From May 13th to May 18th, 2014, a powerful low-pressure system, officially named Tamara and Yvette, impacted a substantial region of Southeastern and Central Europe, leading to widespread flooding and numerous landslides. Serbia and Bosnia and Herzegovina experienced the most significant destruction, as the rainfall recorded during this period was the most intense in 120 years of documented meteorological observations. By May 20th, a minimum of 62 individuals had tragically lost their lives due to the inundation, and hundreds of thousands of residents were displaced from their residences. The municipalities of Obrenovac in Serbia and Doboj in Bosnia and Herzegovina reported the highest number of casualties, having been submerged by several-meter-deep waters originating from adjacent rivers. The expansive floodwaters triggered more than 2,000 landslides throughout Southeast Europe, extending the devastation to numerous communities and rural settlements. The torrential downpours activated flash floods and mudflows, which subsequently affected multiple rivers within the drainage basins of the Sava and Morava.

paraphrase: On November 13, 2014, in Argentina, a Cacerolazo known as the 13N was held, targeting President Cristina Fernández de Kirchner. This event was orchestrated through social media platforms. Participants in the protest voiced their opposition to several issues, including the elevated crime rate, the escalating inflation, and various corruption scandals that had come to light. The demonstrators initially convened at the intersection of Santa Fe and Callao streets, from where they proceeded to march towards the Plaza de Mayo. The Plaza de Mayo ultimately attracted the largest contingent of protesters. A robust security presence was established at both the Plaza de Mayo and the Congressional Plaza. It was noted that this particular demonstration drew fewer participants compared to earlier protests like the 8N and the 18A. Jorge Capitanich, who held the position of chief of the cabinet of ministers, publicly dismissed the demonstration, asserting that citizens ought to express their views regarding the government exclusively during the upcoming 2015 elections.

paraphrase: The 2023 Nigerian money crisis originated from a scarcity of physical currency (the naira) and the Nigerian government's effort to compel its citizens into adopting a newly established, government-backed central bank digital currency. This situation resulted in widespread public demonstrations in the middle of February 2023. Initially, Godwin Emefiele, the governor of the Central Bank, attributed the need for the currency redesign to Nigerians accumulating banknotes in their residences. Subsequently, he asserted that this measure would discourage counterfeiters and individuals involved in paying ransoms to kidnappers, additionally framing it as a move toward a society that relies less on physical cash. He further contended that it would contribute to lowering inflation rates. Certain commentators and political figures have suggested that a driving factor behind the introduction of the CBDC and the devaluation of the previous currency was to prevent vote buying, by frustrating politicians who had accumulated significant amounts of the old currency.

paraphrase: The United Nations High Seas Treaty, formally known as the Agreement under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the conservation and sustainable use of marine biological diversity of areas beyond national jurisdiction, and also referred to as the Biodiversity Beyond National Jurisdiction treaty, was ratified by an intergovernmental assembly at the United Nations on March 4, 2023. This agreement serves as a vital component of the broader United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS). It is structured around four primary aims: Firstly, it seeks the creation of marine protected zones to safeguard marine biodiversity. Secondly, it aims to govern the commercial exploitation of marine genetic resources, which possess potential applications in pharmaceutical development, with the goal of ensuring a fair distribution of the advantages derived from them. Thirdly, it strives to ensure impartial availability for research carried out in international waters. Fourthly, it is dedicated to establishing worldwide benchmarks.

original: Since May–June 2022, a series of labour strikes and industrial disputes have occurred in various industries of the United Kingdom's economy as workers walked out over pay and conditions. The strikes took place with rising inflation, and demands for pay increases that would keep pace with this inflation. The strikes began on 15 June 2022 after members of the National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers (RMT) voted to strike over planned changes to their pay and working conditions. They were soon joined by other railway unions, and a series of one-day strikes halted trains in many parts of the British mainland. Train services operated at 20% of normal capacity on strike days. Public support for the strikes was organised through the Enough is Enough! campaign. With
paraphrase: Beginning in May–June 2022, the economy of the United Kingdom experienced a sequence of labor strikes and industrial disagreements across multiple sectors, as employees initiated walkouts to protest issues concerning their compensation and terms of employment. These industrial actions unfolded amidst a period of escalating inflation and were driven by calls for salary increases that would match the rate of this inflation. The initial strikes commenced on June 15, 2022, following a vote by members of the National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers (RMT) to take industrial action in response to proposed modifications to their wages and working arrangements. Subsequently, other railway unions joined these efforts, leading to a succession of one-day strikes that brought train operations to a standstill in numerous regions of the British mainland. On the days these strikes occurred, train services were operational at only 20% of their customary capacity. Public backing for these strikes was coordinated through the "Enough is Enough!" initiative.

original: The Argentine debt restructuring is a process of debt restructuring by Argentina that began on January 14, 2005, and allowed it to resume payment on 76% of the US$82 billion in sovereign bonds that defaulted in 2001 at the depth of the worst economic crisis in the nation's history. A second debt restructuring in 2010 brought the percentage of bonds under some form of repayment to 93%, though ongoing disputes with holdouts remained. Bondholders who participated in the restructuring settled for repayments of around 30% of face value and deferred payment terms, and began to be paid punctually; the value of their nearly worthless bonds also began to rise. The remaining 7% of bondholders were later repaid 25% less then they were demanding, after centre-right and US-aligned leader
paraphrase: Argentina's debt restructuring initiative commenced on January 14, 2005. This process enabled the nation to restart payments on 76% of its US$82 billion in sovereign bonds, which had gone into default in 2001 amidst the most severe economic crisis the country had ever experienced. A subsequent debt restructuring, implemented in 2010, elevated the proportion of bonds under some arrangement of repayment to 93%, notwithstanding persistent disagreements with holdout creditors. Bondholders who took part in this restructuring accepted repayments amounting to approximately 30% of the face value and agreed to deferred payment schedules; subsequently, they began to receive their payments promptly. Concurrently, the value of their previously almost worthless bonds started to appreciate. The outstanding 7% of bondholders eventually received 25% less than their original demands, following the actions of a centre-right, US-aligned leader.

paraphrase: The geostationary communications satellite known as ARSAT-1, developed by the Argentine firm INVAP, is under the operational control of AR-SAT. Its deployment into orbit occurred on October 16, 2014, originating from French Guiana, and was accomplished in conjunction with the Intelsat-30 satellite via an Ariane 5 rocket. The satellite is projected to occupy the geostationary orbital position of 72° West longitude. Notably, ARSAT-1 holds the distinction of being the inaugural geostationary satellite constructed within Latin America. The comprehensive expenditure for this satellite amounted to 270 million US dollars. ARSAT-1 is equipped with a complement of 24 IEEE Ku band (also referred to as NATO J-band) transponders. Specifically, twelve of these transponders will function at a frequency of 36 MHz, eight at 54 MHz, and four at 72 MHz, collectively providing a total bandwidth of 1152 MHz. This satellite is designed to furnish a broad spectrum of services, encompassing telecommunications, data transmission, telephony, and television, with its primary coverage extending across Argentina, Chile, Uruguay, and Paraguay, and including direct broadcast capabilities.

The title of the artwork is a linguistic twist, as "buff" is a colloquial term referring to the act of painting over graffiti. original: Marchetti's constant is the average time spent by a person for commuting each day. Its value is approximately one hour, or half an hour for a one-way trip. It is named after Italian physicist Cesare Marchetti, though Marchetti himself attributed the "one hour" finding to transportation analyst and engineer Yacov Zahavi.Marchetti posits that although forms of urban planning and transport may change, and although some live in villages and others in cities, people gradually adjust their lives to their conditions (including location of their homes relative to their workplace) such that the average travel time stays approximately constant. Ever since Neolithic times, people have kept the average time spent per day for travel the same, even though the distance may increase due to the advancements in the means
paraphrase: The average daily duration a person dedicates to commuting is known as Marchetti's constant. This constant is estimated to be around one hour, meaning a single journey typically takes about thirty minutes. The constant bears the name of Cesare Marchetti, an Italian physicist, even though Marchetti himself credited the "one hour" observation to Yacov Zahavi, a transportation analyst and engineer. Marchetti's hypothesis suggests that despite transformations in urban development and transportation methods, and regardless of whether individuals reside in rural areas or urban centers, people progressively modify their lifestyles to suit their circumstances (including the proximity of their residences to their places of employment). This adaptation ensures that the typical travel time remains relatively unchanged. Throughout history, starting from the Neolithic era, individuals have consistently maintained a consistent average daily travel time, even as improvements in transportation technologies have allowed for greater distances to be covered.

original: The 16th Congress of the Popular Front for the Liberation of Saguia El Hamra and Río de Oro (Arabic: المؤتمر السادس عشر للجبهة الشعبية لتحرير الساقية الحمراء ووادي الذهب, Spanish: XVI Congreso del Frente Popular para la Liberación de Saguia El Hamra y Río de Oro) was held in the Dajla refugee camp in Tindouf, Algeria between 13 and 22 January 2023 to renovate the governing bodies of the POLISARIO Front and establish the movement's main lines of action and strategy for the next leadership term. The Congress was also used to elect a new General-Secretary for the POLISARIO Front, which would automatically become the President of the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic. For the first time ever, the candidate for general-secretary was not agreed upon before the Congress.
paraphrase: The 16th assembly of the Popular Front for the Liberation of Saguia El Hamra and Río de Oro (Arabic: المؤتمر السادس عشر للجبهة الشعبية لتحرير الساقية الحمراء ووادي الذهب, Spanish: XVI Congreso del Frente Popular para la Liberación de Saguia El Hamra y Río de Oro) convened in the Dajla refugee camp, located in Tindouf, Algeria, from January 13 to January 22, 2023. The primary objectives of this gathering were to overhaul the leadership structures of the POLISARIO Front and to define the core operational guidelines and strategic direction for the organization's subsequent period of governance. Furthermore, the Congress served as the forum for selecting a new General-Secretary for the POLISARIO Front. This individual would, by virtue of their election, automatically assume the presidency of the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic. Notably, this particular Congress marked a historic departure from previous practice, as it was the inaugural instance where the nominee for general-secretary had not been predetermined or mutually agreed upon in advance of the assembly.

Among the provisions are enhanced backing for environmentally friendly transportation, renewable energy sources, and a new levy designated as the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism. This tariff will be applied to emissions associated with high-carbon goods imported from nations that have not implemented adequate greenhouse gas reduction strategies domestically. Furthermore, the plan suggests broadening the scope of the European Union Emissions Trading System to encompass the transport and heating sectors. When contrasted with the net-zero projection developed by the International Energy Agency, this particular plan incorporates a greater number of strategies aimed at guaranteeing that energy costs remain manageable. However, the environmental advocacy group Greenpeace has expressed disapproval of the package, contending that it is inadequate for effectively stopping global warming.

paraphrase: The Euromaidan, also known as the Maidan Uprising, was a period of significant public demonstrations and societal turmoil that originated in Ukraine. This movement commenced on November 21, 2013, characterized by extensive protests held in Kyiv's Maidan Nezalezhnosti, commonly known as Independence Square. paraphrase: The 2013–14 North American winter proved to be exceptionally impactful across the United States, largely attributable to the disruption of the polar vortex in November 2013. This atmospheric event facilitated the southward displacement of extremely frigid air into the United States, initiating a prolonged phase of exceptionally low temperatures. This meteorological trend persisted with minimal interruption throughout the winter season. A multitude of substantial winter storms impacted the Eastern United States, with a particularly prominent event occurring in mid-February, when a powerful winter storm deposited significant amounts of ice and snow across both the Southeastern and Northeastern United States. The majority of the severe cold weather subsided by the conclusion of March, although the Western United States experienced a few additional winter storms as the season drew to a close. While a universally accepted start date for the winter season remains elusive, the events of this particular year were noteworthy.

original: World news or international news or even foreign coverage is the news media jargon for news from abroad, about a country or a global subject. For journalism, it is a branch that deals with news either sent by foreign correspondents or news agencies, or – more recently – information that is gathered or researched through distance communication technologies, such as telephone, satellite TV or the internet. Although in most of the English-speaking world this field is not usually regarded as a specific specialization for journalists, it is so in nearly all the world. Particularly in the United States, there is a blurred distinction between world news and "national" news when they include directly the national government or national institutions, such as wars in which the US are involved
paraphrase: International news, often referred to as world news or foreign coverage within news media terminology, denotes reports originating from other countries or pertaining to global topics. In the realm of journalism, this constitutes a particular area focused on information disseminated by foreign correspondents or news organizations. More recently, it also encompasses data collected or investigated using remote communication tools like telephones, satellite television, or the internet. While in the majority of English-speaking nations, this field is typically not considered a distinct area of expertise for journalists, it holds that status in virtually every other part of the world. Notably, within the United States, the line between world news and "national" news becomes less distinct when the reporting directly involves the national government or national organizations, such as in conflicts where the U.S. is a participant.

This conference was jointly organized by the Pakistani Government and the United Nations, and it took place on January 9, 2023, in Geneva, Switzerland. paraphrase: The Brazilian Anti-Corruption Act, formally known as "Law No. 12,846" and frequently referred to as the Clean Company Act (Lei da Empresa Limpa), is a legislative measure implemented in Brazil in 2014. Its primary objective is to combat corrupt activities among companies operating within Brazil. This law establishes civil and administrative sanctions, and it also provides for potential reductions in these penalties for businesses that collaborate with law enforcement agencies through a formal leniency agreement. Such an agreement must be in writing and mutually accepted by both the business entity and the government. The scope of this legislation is limited to juridical persons, encompassing corporations and various other organizations, but it does not extend to individuals, who are addressed by separate legal frameworks. This Act has been applied on many occasions, leading to leniency agreements that have channeled billions of reals back into the Brazilian Treasury. A particularly notable instance is the agreement reached with Odebrecht S.A., which on its own accounted for a substantial portion of these returns.

original: The Kasta Tomb (Greek: Τύμβος Καστά), also known as the Amphipolis Tomb (Greek: Τάφος της Αμφίπολης), is the largest ancient tumulus (burial mound) ever discovered in Greece, and by comparison dwarfs that of Philip II of Macedon, father of Alexander the Great, in Vergina.It is an ancient Macedonian tomb of the last quarter of the 4th c. BC, and is enclosed in the Kasta mound near the ancient city of Amphipolis, Central Macedonia, in northern Greece. The first excavations at the mound in 1964 led to exposure of the perimeter wall, and further excavations in the 1970s uncovered many other ancient remains. The inner tomb was first discovered in 2012 and entered in August 2014.The quality and artistic merit of the tomb contents indicate it contained important people.
paraphrase: The Kasta Tomb, which is also referred to as the Amphipolis Tomb (Greek: Τάφος της Αμφίπολης), holds the distinction of being the most expansive ancient tumulus, or burial mound, ever unearthed in Greece. Its sheer size significantly overshadows the tomb belonging to Philip II of Macedon, the father of Alexander the Great, located in Vergina. This ancient Macedonian burial site dates back to the final quarter of the 4th century BC and is situated within the Kasta mound, close to the ancient city of Amphipolis in Central Macedonia, a region in northern Greece. Initial archaeological digs at the mound in 1964 resulted in the unearthing of the perimeter wall. Subsequent excavations throughout the 1970s brought to light numerous additional ancient artifacts and structures. The interior of the tomb was first found in 2012, with entry into its chambers occurring in August 2014. The superior craftsmanship and aesthetic value of the items found within the tomb strongly suggest that it served as the final resting place for individuals of significant status.

original: An online newsroom (also known as a pressroom, mediaroom, press center or media center) is a website, web page or site section that contains distributable information about a corporation or organization. The online newsroom was initially created for corporate communicators and public relations firms to target traditional media outlets, fundamentally newspapers, magazines, radio stations and television stations. Multiple public relations audience interests are now supported, including media relations, investor and analyst relations, community relations, and consumer social media relations. Most online newsrooms provide press releases and kits, photographs, logos, audio, video, executive biographies, contact information, events, company history and awards and honors. paraphrase: A digital news center (sometimes referred to as a pressroom, mediaroom, press center, or media center) is a dedicated section, page, or entire website that hosts sharable information pertaining to a company or association. Initially, the digital news center was developed with the purpose of assisting corporate communication professionals and public relations agencies in reaching conventional media channels, specifically newspapers, periodicals, radio broadcasters, and television networks. Currently, a diverse range of public relations stakeholder interests are accommodated, encompassing media engagement, interactions with investors and financial analysts, community outreach, and consumer-focused social media engagement. The majority of digital news centers offer press releases and comprehensive media kits, images, brand logos, sound clips, video content, profiles of executives, contact details, schedules of events, corporate timelines, and accolades. Occasionally, they also feature news feeds relevant to their industry. There is a growing trend among companies to consolidate their various social media presences directly within their digital news centers. Certain news centers might also facilitate other functions.

original: The 2014 United Nations Security Council election was held on 16 October 2014 during the 69th session of the United Nations General Assembly, held at United Nations Headquarters in New York City. The elections were for five non-permanent seats on the UN Security Council for two-year mandates commencing on 1 January 2015. In accordance with the Security Council's rotation rules, whereby the ten non-permanent UNSC seats rotate among the various regional blocs into which UN member states traditionally divide themselves for voting and representation purposes, the five available seats were allocated as follows: One for Africa One for the Asia-Pacific Group One for Latin America and the Caribbean Two for the Western European and Others GroupThe five members will serve on the Security Council for the 2015–16 period.
paraphrase: On October 16, 2014, the United Nations General Assembly, in its 69th session at the United Nations Headquarters in New York City, conducted the 2014 United Nations Security Council election. This electoral process was organized to fill five non-permanent positions on the UN Security Council, with the successful candidates serving two-year terms that would begin on January 1, 2015. Adhering to the established rotation guidelines of the Security Council, which dictate that the ten non-permanent UNSC seats are distributed cyclically among the distinct regional blocs — these blocs being the traditional divisions used by UN member states for electoral and representational functions — the five vacant seats were distributed as follows: one seat was designated for the African bloc, another for the Asia-Pacific Group, a third for the Latin American and Caribbean region, and two seats were allotted to the Western European and Others Group. These five newly elected members are slated to serve on the Security Council for the duration of the 2015–16 term.

paraphrase: The armed conflict in Las Anod, which began in 2023, is an active confrontation involving the Somaliland National Army and various clan militias, specifically from the Dhulbahante, Majeerteen, and Warsangali groups. This conflict is centered in Las Anod, the principal city of the Sool region. The hostilities commenced on February 6, following a forceful suppression of civilian demonstrations by Somaliland's security forces. Subsequently, on February 8, Garad Jama Garad Ali, who holds the esteemed position of supreme Garad for the Dhulbahante, announced his intention for his clan to separate from Somaliland and rejoin the Federal Government of Somalia. This conflict has resulted in a tragic loss of more than 300 lives and has caused the displacement of between 153,000 and 203,000 individuals. These refugees have either sought refuge outside the immediate conflict zone, moving towards Buuhodle and the border with Ethiopia, or have been displaced internally to areas under the control of SSC-Khatumo Forces, such as Taleex, Xudun, Buuhodle, among others, and to cities within Puntland, including Garowe, Galkacyo, and Burtinle. Reports on March 16 indicated that Somaliland troops were engaging in shelling attacks directed at civilians from the area.

original: The Abkhazian Revolution took place in 2014, when President Aleksandr Ankvab resigned after hundreds of demonstrators stormed his office. After mass protests in the capital Sukhumi and the occupation of his office on 27 May, Ankvab fled to his hometown of Gudauta and ultimately resigned on 1 June, after previously denouncing the demonstration as an attempted coup d'état.The uprising was attributed to public anger with Ankvab over his perceived liberal policy toward ethnic Georgians in Abkhazia, a breakaway republic with limited recognition. Although Abkhazia seceded from Georgia in 1992, the Ankvab administration allowed ethnic Georgians to register as voters and receive Abkhazian passports.The revolution led to an early presidential election being called in August 2014. Opposition leader Raul Khajimba was elected president with a narrow majority of the
paraphrase: In 2014, a significant political upheaval, known as the Abkhazian Revolution, occurred, culminating in the resignation of President Aleksandr Ankvab. This event was triggered by hundreds of protestors storming his official workplace. Following extensive demonstrations in Sukhumi, the capital city, and the subsequent takeover of his office on May 27th, Ankvab sought refuge in Gudauta, his place of birth. He eventually stepped down from his position on June 1st, having previously characterized the public demonstration as an effort to overthrow the government. The primary cause of this insurgency was attributed to widespread public dissatisfaction with Ankvab, stemming from his seemingly liberal approach towards ethnic Georgians residing in Abkhazia, a self-declared republic with limited international recognition. Despite Abkhazia's separation from Georgia in 1992, Ankvab's government had permitted ethnic Georgians to register for voting and obtain Abkhazian passports. As a direct consequence of this revolution, an early presidential election was scheduled for August 2014. During this election, Raul Khajimba, a prominent figure in the opposition, secured the presidency by a slim margin.

paraphrase: On January 15, 2023, the 28th Critics' Choice Awards ceremony took place at the Fairmont Century Plaza Hotel located in Los Angeles, California. This event served to recognize and celebrate the most outstanding accomplishments in both cinematic and television productions throughout the year 2022. The CW network aired the ceremony, which was presided over by Chelsea Handler. She assumed the role of host, succeeding Taye Diggs, who had held the hosting duties for the preceding four years in a row. Following the pattern established in the two years prior, the nominations for film and television categories were revealed independently. The nominations specifically for television programs were made public on December 6, 2022. Subsequently, the nominations for films were announced on December 14, 2022. Among the film nominees, "Everything Everywhere All at Once" garnered the highest number of nominations, receiving 14. This achievement matched a record previously set by "The Shape of Water" (2017), "The Favourite" (2018), and "The Irishman" (2019). original: Simon Hall (1977 or 1978 – 23 February 2014) was a British murderer who was wrongly helped by 'miscarriage of justice' campaigners, only for him to go on to confess to the murder he was convicted of and prove he was rightly convicted. Hall stabbed 79-year-old pensioner Joan Albert to death in her home in Capel St Mary, Suffolk in 2001, and was convicted of her murder two years later. Subsequently, the high-profile miscarriage of justice programme Rough Justice took up his case and aired a programme campaigning for him. Several MPs, Bristol University's 'Innocence Project' campaign group, his mother and girlfriend were also deceived into campaigning for him, and the Criminal Cases Review Commission (which had itself been set up in response to Rough Justice in 1997)
paraphrase: Simon Hall (born in either 1977 or 1978 and deceased on February 23, 2014) was a British individual found guilty of murder. He received erroneous support from activists advocating for those believed to be victims of wrongful conviction. However, he subsequently admitted to the homicide for which he had been found guilty, thereby confirming the legitimacy of his conviction. Hall fatally stabbed Joan Albert, a 79-year-old retiree, at her residence in Capel St Mary, Suffolk, in 2001. He was subsequently found guilty of her murder in 2003. Following his conviction, the prominent television program "Rough Justice," which focuses on miscarriages of justice, took an interest in his case and broadcast an episode lobbying on his behalf. A number of Members of Parliament, the "Innocence Project" advocacy organization at Bristol University, his mother, and his girlfriend were also misled into supporting his cause. The Criminal Cases Review Commission, an entity established in 1997 partly in response to the original "Rough Justice" program, also became involved.

paraphrase: On April 26, 2023, approximately at 6:10 PM EDT, an explosive device, subsequently identified as an improvised explosive device, detonated in Weare, New Hampshire. This incident occurred at a residence situated on NH Route 77. In response to the explosion, personnel from the ATF and FBI were dispatched to the location. The following morning, at 6:50 AM, a second bomb detonated within a van that was parked on Sugar Hill Road. The individual operating the van stated that he was in the process of driving when he observed the bomb. He then pulled the vehicle over with the intention of removing the device, at which point it exploded directly in front of him, resulting in minor injuries. Local residents residing within a two-mile radius of either of these bombing incidents have received a request to examine their surveillance recordings for any potentially suspicious activity. On April 27, 2023, Dale Stewart was apprehended in Webster, and a pipe bomb discovered inside his vehicle was successfully neutralized. He subsequently underwent arraignment.

paraphrase: On April 6, numerous rockets, numbering in the dozens, were launched from Lebanon, targeting Israel and resulting in injuries to 3 Israeli non-combatants. The Israel Defense Forces (IDF) declared that it had successfully intercepted 25 rockets originating from Lebanon. Prior to this, alert systems had been activated in the municipality of Shlomi and in Betzet, situated in the northern region of Israel. Israeli authorities attributed the rocket launches to the Palestinian groups Hamas and PIJ, asserting that these actions had received Hezbollah's endorsement. During the early hours of April 7, the Israeli Air Force conducted retaliatory strikes, targeting locations in Tyre, Lebanon, and within the Gaza Strip. The United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL), through an official written declaration, characterized the prevailing circumstances as "exceptionally grave" and called for de-escalation. It further stated that Aroldo Lazaro, the head of UNIFIL, was engaged in communications with officials representing both involved parties. These aggressive actions represent the most significant intensification of conflict between the two nations since the 2006 Lebanon War.

Of the 266 candidates, 22 were women. According to election officials, 349,713 Bahrainis, including 175,998 men and 173,175 women, were listed to vote. paraphrase: In November 2014, general elections were conducted in Bahrain with the purpose of selecting the forty members who would serve on the Council of Representatives. The initial round of voting occurred on November 22nd. A subsequent second round was held on November 29th for the 34 constituencies where no single candidate secured a majority of the votes. The Shiite Islamist opposition group, Al-Wefaq, chose not to participate in these elections. Among the 266 individuals who put themselves forward as candidates, 22 were women. Election authorities reported that a total of 349,713 Bahraini citizens were registered to cast their ballots, comprising 175,998 men and 173,175 women. While the government officially stated that voter turnout reached 52.6%, the opposition contended that the actual figure was merely 30%. Independent candidates emerged victorious, securing 37 out of the 40 available seats. Sunni Islamists experienced a reduction in their representation, losing two of the five seats they previously held. Due to the boycott by Al-Wefaq, the number of Shiite Members of Parliament decreased to 14. Female representation was also impacted.

paraphrase: Beginning in early November 2022, demonstrations commenced at multiple sites across Ireland. These protests emerged following the establishment of temporary refugee shelters in various regions of the nation by the Department of Children, Equality, Disability, Integration and Youth (DCEDIY), in its effort to house the 65,000 refugees who arrived throughout 2022. These demonstrations have taken place in areas such as East Wall, Ballymun, Drimnagh, and other parts of Dublin, as well as in Fermoy and Mallow in County Cork, Kill in County Kildare, Lismore in County Waterford, and Mullingar. The concerns voiced during these protests consistently included perceived discrimination in refugee selection and the failure to provide advance notice to local residents. Furthermore, demonstrators have challenged the absence of women and children in certain accommodation facilities. The involvement of anti-immigrant activists, individuals affiliated with far-right organizations, and violent offenders at these protests has been prominently noted online and through various other channels.

paraphrase: On Thursday, April 20, 2023, a hybrid solar eclipse took place. A solar eclipse happens when the Moon positions itself between our planet, Earth, and the Sun, consequently blocking the Sun either completely or partially from an observer's perspective on Earth. A hybrid solar eclipse represents an infrequent category of solar eclipse, characterized by its transformation from an annular eclipse to a total eclipse and then back again, as the Moon's shadow traverses the Earth's surface. The phenomenon of totality manifests along a slender trajectory across the Earth's expanse, while a partial solar eclipse can be observed within an extensive adjacent area spanning thousands of kilometers. Hybrid solar eclipses are exceedingly uncommon, accounting for merely 3.1% of all solar eclipses observed during the 21st century. For this particular eclipse, the totality phase was observable from the North West Cape peninsula and Barrow Island in Western Australia, as well as the eastern regions of Timor-Leste and West Papua.

This event, termed the 2022 Uganda Ebola outbreak, resulted in the infection of more than 160 individuals, with 77 fatalities among them. This marked the fifth instance of a Sudan ebolavirus outbreak in Uganda's history. The Ministry of Health of Uganda officially announced the commencement of this outbreak on September 20, 2022. paraphrase: An incident that gained international media attention took place in July 2014, involving Pattaramon Janbua, a Thai woman who had been engaged as a surrogate mother for an Australian couple. Ms. Janbua was attempting to secure financial assistance for her surrogate son, who was suffering from a critical illness. The infant had been under her care since his birth in December 2013; his biological parents, David John Farnell and Wenyu Wendy Li, departed Thailand two months subsequently, accompanied by baby Gammy's twin sister, Pipah. Following ultrasound findings seven months into the gestational period, which revealed that Ms. Pattaramon was expecting twins and that one of the twins, a boy, had Down syndrome, Farnell and Li made a request for her to terminate the pregnancy for the male twin, expressing their intention to only retain the child's twin sister. Ms. Pattaramon declined this request, referencing her Buddhist faith as the reason, and instead made the decision to raise the child.

During July of 2014, three individuals of Azerbaijani ethnicity crossed the Nagorno-Karabakh Line of Contact, entering the Kalbajar District, an area then under the control of the secessionist Republic of Artsakh's military. This event was succeeded by the disappearance of Smbat Tsakanyan, a local 17-year-old, whose body was later discovered exhibiting gunshot wounds. original: The 2014 Burkina Faso uprising was a series of demonstrations and riots in Burkina Faso in October 2014 that quickly spread to multiple cities. They began in response to attempts at changing the constitution to allow President Blaise Compaoré to run again and extend his 27 years in office. Pressure for political change came from civil society and in particular from the country's youth. Following a tumultuous day on 30 October, which included the involvement of former Defence Minister Kouamé Lougué and the burning of the National Assembly and other government buildings as well as the ruling Congress for Democracy and Progress party's headquarters, Compaoré dissolved the government and declared a state of emergency before eventually fleeing to Côte d'Ivoire with the support of President Alassane Ouattara. These inundations impacted seven provinces, specifically the Eastern Cape, Northern Cape, Mpumalanga, KwaZulu-Natal, and North West regions. The deluges resulted in human fatalities and significant destruction to residential properties, commercial establishments, essential infrastructure, roadways, and bridges, while also harming agricultural crops and livestock. Jack Teixeira, an airman first class of the Massachusetts Air National Guard, had allegedly photographed printouts of the documents at his parents' home in Dighton, Massachusetts and posted them to the instant messaging platform Discord on a server named "Thug Shaker Central". paraphrase: During April of 2023, two distinct collections of confidential foreign intelligence records belonging to the United States started to appear across various online platforms such as Twitter, Telegram, and 4chan. It was alleged that Jack Teixeira, an airman first class serving in the Massachusetts Air National Guard, took pictures of printed copies of these documents while at his parents' residence in Dighton, Massachusetts. He then uploaded these images to the instant messaging service Discord, specifically onto a server identified as "Thug Shaker Central." The initial uploads of these materials can be traced back to October 2022. The content of these documents predominantly pertained to the conflict between Russia and Ukraine, yet they also encompassed evaluations of foreign intelligence regarding countries like North Korea, China, Iran, and the United Arab Emirates. A portion of these documents was subsequently distributed to Discord servers dedicated to a particular YouTuber and the open-world video game Minecraft, occurring between late February and early March of 2023. In April, a user on 4chan disseminated these documents.

Paul Janovitz is the younger brother of Bill Janovitz, lead singer and songwriter of Buffalo Tom. paraphrase: Cold Water Flat, an American musical group, was established in 1990. Its founding members were Paul Janovitz, who handled vocals and guitar, Ted Silva on bass, and Paul Harding on drums. These three individuals came together to form the band while they were students at the University of Massachusetts Amherst. Notably, Paul Janovitz is the younger sibling of Bill Janovitz, who is recognized as the lead vocalist and primary songwriter for the band Buffalo Tom. The band initially started performing live shows in the Boston area. However, their future became uncertain when Janovitz relocated to New York, leading the group to the brink of disbanding. Their plans changed when Play It Again Sam, a European record label, extended an offer to release one of their albums, prompting them to rethink their decision to split. Subsequently, in 1995, they entered into a recording contract with MCA Records. Under this new agreement, they released their second album, which was self-titled and recorded at Fort Apache Studios. Despite receiving some radio play, primarily on college radio stations across the United States, the band was unable to achieve broader public recognition. Not long after this, the group dissolved, and Janovitz subsequently joined the band Dragstrip.

Popular demonstrations in Phnom Penh took place against the government of Prime Minister Hun Sen, triggered by widespread allegations of electoral fraud during the Cambodian general election of 2013. paraphrase: A series of demonstrations against the government (Khmer: បាតុកម្មប្រឆាំងរាជរដ្ឋាភិបាល) occurred in Cambodia, spanning from July 2013 to July 2014. These widely-supported protests, primarily in Phnom Penh, were directed at Prime Minister Hun Sen's administration. The catalyst for these protests was extensive accusations of electoral fraud following the Cambodian general election in 2013. Additional factors fueling the unrest included calls for an increase in the minimum wage to $160 monthly and public dissatisfaction regarding Vietnamese involvement in Cambodia. The primary opposition party opted out of parliamentary participation subsequent to the elections, leading to significant protests throughout December 2013. A government intervention in January 2014 resulted in four fatalities and the dismantling of the primary protest encampment. It is plausible that the Cambodian protests were partially instigated by a prevailing sense of corruption, limited liberties, and inadequate standards of living.

original: Japanese whaling, in terms of active hunting of whales, is estimated by the Japan Whaling Association to have begun around the 12th century. However, Japanese whaling on an industrial scale began around the 1890s when Japan started to participate in the modern whaling industry, at that time an industry in which many countries participated. Japan resumed commercial whaling in July 2019, and since then whaling activities have been confined to its territorial waters and exclusive economic zone.During the 20th century, Japan was heavily involved in commercial whaling. This continued until the International Whaling Commission (IWC) moratorium on commercial whaling went into effect in 1986. Japan continued to hunt whales using the scientific research provision in the agreement and Japanese whaling was conducted by the Institute of Cetacean Research.
paraphrase: The Japan Whaling Association estimates that the active pursuit of whales by the Japanese, involving their direct hunting, commenced approximately in the 12th century. Nevertheless, large-scale Japanese whaling operations, characterized by industrial methods, originated around the 1890s as Japan became involved in the contemporary whaling sector, an industry in which numerous nations were then engaged. Japan recommenced commercial whaling in July 2019, and ever since, its whaling endeavors have been restricted to its territorial seas and its exclusive economic zone. Throughout the 20th century, Japan played a significant role in the commercial whaling industry. This involvement persisted until 1986, when the International Whaling Commission's (IWC) prohibition on commercial whaling became effective. Japan proceeded with whale hunting under the provision for scientific research within the agreement, and the Institute of Cetacean Research carried out Japanese whaling activities.

paraphrase: A plebiscite took place on February 6, 2023, across several administrative divisions within Ethiopia's Southern Nations, Nationalities, and Peoples' Region (SNNP). These divisions included the Wolayita, Gamo, Gofa, South Omo, Gedeo, and Konso Zones, alongside the special woredas of Dirashe, Amaro, Burji, Ale, and Basketo. The purpose of this electoral process was to determine if these specified areas desired to secede from the SNNP Region and establish an independent administrative Region of their own. This electoral event is the third of its kind, building upon two prior referendums conducted in 2019 and 2021 in different parts of what was then the SNNP. In both of those earlier instances, the outcome was a decision to separate and establish new regions. While the recent referendum received preliminary approval, a caveat emerged regarding the Wolayita Zone, which is mandated to conduct its voting anew due to the identification of procedural anomalies. Conversely, the remaining zones and woredas that participated successfully passed their respective motions to secede in July 2023, a decision subsequently communicated to the national House of Federation.

This crisis is intrinsically linked to a political discourse within Congress concerning federal government expenditures and the accumulating national debt of the U.S. government. paraphrase: The 65th yearly Grammy Awards gala, an event dedicated to recognizing musical achievements, took place on February 5, 2023, at the Crypto.com Arena in Los Angeles. This ceremony honored the top recordings, musical arrangements, and performers from the qualifying period spanning October 1, 2021, through September 30, 2022. The selection process was carried out by members of the National Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences. The list of nominees was publicly revealed on November 15, 2022. Trevor Noah, a comedian from South Africa who previously presided over both the 63rd and 64th ceremonies, once again assumed the role of host. Beyoncé garnered the highest number of nominations, receiving nine, and achieved a tie with Maverick City Music for the most wins, each securing four. Kendrick Lamar followed with eight nominations and three victories, while Adele and Brandi Carlile each accumulated seven nominations. Beyoncé, with a cumulative total of 88 nominations throughout her career, reached an equal standing with her husband, Jay-Z, as the individual with the most nominations.

original: Severe Tropical Cyclone Gabrielle was a severe tropical cyclone that devastated the North Island of New Zealand and affected parts of Vanuatu and Australia in February 2023. This event took place at the Dipoli Hall, located in the Otakaari 1 Building, within the Aalto University campus in Espoo, Finland. Aalto University played a dual role, serving as both the host and a co-organizer for the ceremony. This gathering served as the inaugural activity leading up to the Aalto University Academic Summit, an event specifically focused on "The Impact of Universities on Economic Growth." During the ceremony, the WCC recognized two prominent scientists with awards. The highly respected enzymologist, Professor Sir Philip Cohen, was honored with the Albert Einstein World Award of Science. This award acknowledged his four decades of dedicated research into the intricacies of cellular communication. Furthermore, Professor Federico Rosei was presented with the José Vasconcelos World Award of Education, an accolade bestowed upon him for his exceptional contributions to university teaching. Jussi Nuorteva, who holds the position of Director of the National Archives of Finland and is also a member of the WCC, had the distinction of presenting the Winners' Book to the esteemed laureates, including Professor Sir Philip.

paraphrase: The 2023 DFB-Pokal Final is set to determine the victor of the 2022–23 DFB-Pokal, which marks the 80th installment of Germany's yearly football cup competition. This championship game is scheduled for June 3, 2023, and will take place at Berlin's Olympiastadion. The team that emerges victorious will be granted the privilege of hosting the 2023 DFL-Supercup at the commencement of the subsequent season. They will then compete against the champion of the 2022–23 Bundesliga season. An exception arises if the same team manages to secure both the Bundesliga title and the DFB-Pokal, achieving what is known as a "double." In such an event, the Bundesliga runner-up will step in to fill that role and host the DFL-Supercup. Furthermore, the DFB-Pokal winner secures a direct entry into the group stage of the 2023–24 UEFA Europa League. paraphrase: The gathering of Western Balkan nations, a conference held on August 28, 2014, brought together the leaders of states and governments from the Western Balkans area, an event first proposed by German Chancellor Angela Merkel. The concept for arranging this conference emerged from the commemoration of the centenary of the outbreak of World War I. This particular conference represents the inaugural event within this framework, and the organizers conveyed their aspiration for it to become an ongoing tradition, with Vienna and Paris identified as prospective subsequent host cities following Berlin (an initiative known as the Berlin Process). Günther Oettinger, during the conference, affirmed that this event would be held yearly, with Vienna slated to host in 2015 and Paris in 2016. The German Chancellor had previously declared her intention to arrange such a conference on June 7, 2014. Invitations to participate were extended to every nation in the region.

paraphrase: The anti-terrorism legislation currently under consideration by the Swedish parliament is known as the Swedish anti-terrorism bill of 2023. This proposed legal framework, which initially emerged in 2017 after the Stockholm truck attack, aims to criminalize "the participation in a terrorist organisation." Sweden has previously explored implementing anti-terrorism laws; however, these earlier attempts faced considerable resistance from human rights advocates. Although the legislation is not yet finalized, it received renewed impetus subsequent to the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Following this event, Sweden formally sought NATO membership, but its application encountered objections from Turkey. Turkey’s opposition was based on the assertion that Sweden and Finland (who submitted their applications concurrently) "host terrorist organisations which act against Turkey," specifically mentioning groups such as the PKK, PYD, YPG, and the Gülen movement. It is noteworthy that the PKK holds recognition as a terrorist organization by several international bodies.

original: The 2023 Chinese military exercises around Taiwan (simplified Chinese: 2023年环台军事演练; traditional Chinese: 2023年環台軍事演練) are a series of ongoing military exercises by the People's Liberation Army (PLA) of the People's Republic of China (PRC) encircling Taiwan (the Republic of China, ROC). On 8 April 2023, the PLA announced the start of "combat readiness patrols" and dispatched dozens of fighter jets and several warships toward Taiwan. The exercises occurred in response to Taiwanese president Tsai Ing-wen's visit to US Speaker of the House Kevin McCarthy.On 4 March 2023, McCarthy announced he had planned a meeting with president Tsai, which was scheduled for early April. In response, the PLA made multiple threats toward the Taiwanese government. Tsai arrived in the U.S. on 4 April and met with prominent lawmakers, including McCarthy.
paraphrase: The People's Liberation Army (PLA) of the People's Republic of China (PRC) is currently conducting a sequence of military drills, known as the 2023 Chinese military exercises around Taiwan (simplified Chinese: 2023年环台军事演练; traditional Chinese: 2023年環台軍事演練), which involve the encirclement of Taiwan (the Republic of China, ROC). These exercises commenced on April 8, 2023, when the PLA declared the initiation of "combat readiness patrols" and deployed numerous fighter jets and multiple warships in the direction of Taiwan. The impetus for these drills was Taiwanese president Tsai Ing-wen's visit to meet with US Speaker of the House Kevin McCarthy. Preceding this, on March 4, 2023, McCarthy had disclosed his intention to hold a meeting with President Tsai, which was slated for early April. In anticipation of this, the PLA issued several warnings to the Taiwanese government. President Tsai subsequently arrived in the United States on April 4 and engaged in discussions with notable legislators, including Speaker McCarthy.

paraphrase: On October 12, 2014, Bosnia and Herzegovina conducted its general elections. These elections determined the composition of the country's Presidency, along with the national, entity, and cantonal administrations. A total of 54.4% of eligible voters participated. The elections for the House of Representatives were bifurcated: one segment was for the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina, and the other was for Republika Srpska. During the presidential election, citizens in the Federation once again chose Bosniak Bakir Izetbegović and selected Croat Dragan Čović, whereas those in Republika Srpska opted for Serb Mladen Ivanić. The Party of Democratic Action secured the most seats in the House of Representatives, obtaining 10 out of 42. Although Bakir Izetbegović, the Bosniak representative, was the sole incumbent member of the three-person Presidency to retain his position, Mladen Ivanić, the Serb representative, was chosen to join.

In March 2016, Barack Obama became the first U.S. president to visit Cuba since Calvin Coolidge, in 1928.On December 17, 2014, U.S. President Barack Obama and Cuban leader Raúl Castro announced the beginning of the process of normalizing relations between Cuba and the United States. The normalization agreement was secretly negotiated in preceding months, facilitated by Pope Francis and largely hosted by the Government of Canada. Meetings were held in both Canada and Vatican City. The agreement would see the lifting of some U.S. travel restrictions, fewer restrictions on remittances, U.S. banks' access to the Cuban financial system, and the reopening
paraphrase: The normalization of diplomatic ties between Cuba and the United States, known as the Cuban thaw (Spanish: Deshielo cubano), commenced in December 2014, bringing an end to 54 years of animosity between the two countries. Notably, in March 2016, Barack Obama made history as the inaugural U.S. president to set foot in Cuba since Calvin Coolidge's visit in 1928. The initiation of this process was publicly announced on December 17, 2014, by U.S. President Barack Obama and Cuban leader Raúl Castro, who declared their intent to normalize relations between Cuba and the United States. This normalization accord was the culmination of clandestine negotiations that had transpired over several months prior, with crucial assistance from Pope Francis and significant logistical support provided by the Government of Canada. Discussions took place in both Canada and Vatican City. As part of this agreement, certain U.S. travel restrictions were slated for removal, limitations on remittances were to be eased, U.S. financial institutions would gain entry to Cuba's financial infrastructure, and the reopening of embassies was planned.

paraphrase: The legal proceedings known as Karen Petrosyan v. Azerbaijan focused on the human rights of Artush Petrosyan and his offspring, Karen Petrosyan. Karen Petrosyan, an Armenian citizen hailing from Chinari village within Armenia's Tavush province, tragically perished while being held captive in Azerbaijan during 2014. The European Court of Human Rights rendered its verdict on this matter, following an application (numbered 32427/16) that was brought against the Republic of Azerbaijan. This application was officially filed with the Court on April 25, 2016, by Artush Petrosyan, in accordance with the stipulations of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms. Artush Petrosyan asserted that his son had endured public humiliation, torture, and ultimately, death by decapitation, at the hands of Azerbaijani State operatives. Furthermore, he claimed that the repatriation of his son's remains had been unduly delayed, and that no comprehensive or effective inquiry had been conducted regarding these grave circumstances.

original: In the afternoon of January 21, 2014, the Chinese internet suffered a major failure. The country's DNS infrastructure, which is responsible for translating domain names into IP addresses, started directing unrelated domains from various TLDs to the completely unresponsive IP address 65.49.2.178 at 15:10 (UTC+8). As a result, two-thirds of all domestic websites became non-functional, including such high-traffic sites as Baidu and Sina.It is debated what caused this incident. Chinese officials point to the fact that the IP address is owned by Dynamic Internet Technology, an American Falun Gong-affiliated corporation most known for developing the Great Firewall circumvention tool Freegate, and argue that it was caused by external hacking. Independent researchers, however, argue that the incident is more likely caused by a misconfiguration in Great Firewall's DNS poisoning
paraphrase:
On the afternoon of January 21, 2014, a substantial breakdown afflicted the Chinese internet. At precisely 15:10 (UTC+8), the nation's Domain Name System (DNS) infrastructure, which serves the critical function of converting human-readable domain names into numerical IP addresses, began rerouting a multitude of unconnected domains across various Top-Level Domains (TLDs) to the entirely unresponsive IP address 65.49.2.178. This malfunction led to the inoperability of two-thirds of all domestic websites, encompassing prominent and heavily trafficked platforms like Baidu and Sina.

The precise origin of this occurrence remains a subject of contention. Official Chinese sources emphasize that the IP address in question belongs to Dynamic Internet Technology, an American company with ties to Falun Gong, widely recognized for creating Freegate, a tool designed to bypass the Great Firewall. These officials contend that the incident was the outcome of an external hacking attempt. Conversely, independent investigators propose that the event more plausibly stemmed from a misconfiguration within the Great Firewall's DNS poisoning mechanism.

paraphrase: An office dedicated to the collection or dissemination of news is known as a news bureau. Corresponding terms are employed for particular bureaus, frequently to denote a geographical area or the extent of their reporting. For instance, a "Tokyo bureau" designates a specific news organization's branch office situated in Tokyo; a "foreign bureau" is a general descriptor for a news office established in a nation different from the main operational hub; and a "Washington bureau" signifies an office, generally located in Washington, D.C., that reports on events pertaining to national politics within the United States. The individual who manages a news bureau is commonly referred to as the bureau chief. This terminology is differentiated from a news desk, which pertains to the editorial responsibility of allocating reporters and other personnel, and generally orchestrating news narratives, and sometimes even the physical workstation where these tasks are performed.
